items.title: GitHub Confirms 3,800 Internal Repositories Stolen via Poisoned VS Code Extension
items.summary: GitHub confirmed that the TeamPCP hacking group exfiltrated approximately 3,800 internal repositories by poisoning the Nx Console VS Code extension — an extension active on the Visual Studio Marketplace for only 18 minutes before being detected. A GitHub employee installed the malicious extension, which silently transmitted internal source code for AI projects including GitHub Copilot, Codex, CodeQL, and Actions workflows. GitHub said customer data stored outside internal repositories was not impacted, though TeamPCP is now attempting to sell the stolen AI tooling code for $50,000.
keyConcepts: VS Code extension supply chain attack
items.whyItMatters: The GitHub breach shows that AI development infrastructure — model tooling, agent frameworks, and Copilot internals — is now a high-value target for organized cybercrime. Developers who rely on VS Code extensions and third-party packages face growing supply chain risk, particularly when their codebase touches frontier AI APIs and model weights.

knowledgeChecks.question: How did TeamPCP gain access to GitHub's internal repositories?
options: By brute-forcing GitHub employee credentials using a stolen password list
By exploiting a remote code execution vulnerability in GitHub Actions
By poisoning a VS Code extension that a GitHub employee installed on their machine
By compromising GitHub's cloud infrastructure through an unpatched API endpoint
knowledgeChecks.explanation: TeamPCP poisoned the Nx Console VS Code extension — injecting malware that silently ran on the developer's machine and exfiltrated access to internal GitHub repositories. The malicious version was live on the Visual Studio Marketplace for only 18 minutes, but that was enough for one employee to install it before GitHub removed it. This is a textbook developer supply chain attack: compromise a trusted tool, not the target system itself.

knowledgeChecks.question: Why does stealing AI tooling source code (like Copilot internals) represent a different threat profile than stealing application code?
options: AI tooling code is legally protected under different copyright standards than application code
Stolen AI tooling exposes model architectures, prompt engineering techniques, and safety filters — potentially enabling adversaries to replicate or circumvent them
AI tooling code is inherently more valuable simply because it involves machine learning
Regulatory requirements mandate that AI tooling breaches be disclosed within 24 hours
knowledgeChecks.explanation: When adversaries steal the internals of AI tools like Copilot or Codex, they gain insight into prompt construction, safety filtering logic, and model orchestration patterns. This intelligence can help competitors replicate capabilities faster, help attackers craft adversarial inputs that evade safety measures, or expose model behavior in ways that the vendor has deliberately kept confidential — threats that go far beyond what typical source code theft enables.

items.title: Anthropic Project Glasswing Update: AI-Powered Security Scan Finds 10,000+ Critical Vulnerabilities Across Major Software
items.summary: Anthropic shared a progress update on Project Glasswing, its initiative using Claude Mythos Preview to help organizations scan critical software for vulnerabilities. The program has now surfaced more than 10,000 high-or-critical-severity bugs across partner organizations — with some seeing 10x improvements in discovery rate over traditional human-led auditing. Cloudflare identified ~2,000 bugs (400 classified high/critical) with fewer false positives than conventional testing, and Mozilla fixed 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox 150. Anthropic is expanding to additional partners including U.S. and allied government systems, backed by $100M in committed model usage credits.
items.whyItMatters: A 10x improvement in bug discovery rate — if it holds across diverse codebases — represents a structural shift in how software security works. Routine security audits currently take months and cost millions; AI-accelerated scanning compresses this dramatically. Project Glasswing is the most rigorous public evidence yet that frontier models can perform meaningful work in specialized, high-stakes technical domains.

knowledgeChecks.question: What does a '10x improvement in bug discovery rate' in Project Glasswing most likely mean in practice?
options: Claude Mythos found 10 times more total lines of code than human reviewers could read
For the same time investment, AI-assisted security testing surfaced 10 times more valid vulnerabilities than equivalent human-led audits
Anthropic's model is 10 times faster at reading documentation than human security researchers
The AI reduced code review meeting time by a factor of 10 through automated summarization
knowledgeChecks.explanation: A '10x improvement in discovery rate' means that for roughly the same cost and time, the AI-assisted process found approximately 10 times as many real, actionable vulnerabilities as the previous human-only approach. Critically, the results also showed fewer false positives — meaning the AI's findings were higher quality, not just higher volume. This combination (more bugs found, fewer wasted investigation hours) is what makes the improvement economically significant.

knowledgeChecks.question: Why does Anthropic call Claude Mythos Preview 'the most capable and dangerous AI model ever built' while also using it for security research?
options: Anthropic is using Mythos to find exploits in competitor AI systems, not its own

The same deep technical reasoning that makes Mythos capable of discovering hidden vulnerabilities also means it could theoretically be used to create novel exploits — making controlled deployment critical
Mythos is dangerous only in the sense that it requires extremely high compute costs to run
Anthropic is legally required to describe all frontier models as dangerous under new EU regulations
knowledgeChecks.explanation: Frontier models capable of finding subtle, non-obvious vulnerabilities across large codebases possess the same underlying reasoning that could theoretically help adversaries design attacks. This is the dual-use tension at the heart of AI security applications: the more capable the model, the more valuable it is for defense — and the more carefully its deployment must be controlled. Project Glasswing's approach (limited access, $100M in curated credits, no general release) reflects this tradeoff explicitly.

items.title: Leaked Audio: Meta Tracked Employee Keystrokes and Emails to Train AI, Then Laid Off 8,000
items.summary: A leaked recording from a Meta internal all-hands meeting captured Mark Zuckerberg explaining that Meta had been monitoring employee activity — including Gmail, coding sessions in VSCode, and internal tools like Metamate — to generate AI training data for the company's models. The revelation surfaced the same week 8,000 employees received layoff notices that cited AI efficiency gains. Zuckerberg's internal framing described the surveillance as learning from 'really smart people,' while Meta's public messaging positioned the layoffs as routine organizational restructuring.
keyConcepts: AI training data collection from employees
items.whyItMatters: The Meta incident is the clearest example yet of the feedback loop at the heart of AI-driven workforce displacement: companies use employee work to train AI systems that then reduce the need for those same employees. As this pattern becomes more visible, it will reshape how professionals negotiate their employment terms and what data rights workers expect in AI-era workplaces.

knowledgeChecks.question: What distinguishes Meta's employee monitoring program from standard corporate software monitoring?
options: Meta required employees to sign an explicit data-sharing consent form that most didn't read
The monitoring was specifically designed to capture work product and behavioral patterns to train AI models — not merely for security or productivity measurement
Meta used government-grade surveillance software that is banned for private companies in most jurisdictions
Meta only monitored employees who were already on performance improvement plans
knowledgeChecks.explanation: Many companies log software usage for IT security or productivity purposes, but Meta's program was purpose-built to capture how skilled employees think and work in order to train AI systems. The surveillance covered coding behavior, email communication patterns, and internal tool interactions — exactly the types of knowledge work the company hoped AI could eventually replicate. The ethical issue isn't monitoring per se, but using employees as unwitting training data for the systems that then displaced them.

knowledgeChecks.question: What career lesson does the Meta situation most directly illustrate for AI engineers and knowledge workers?
options: Working at large tech companies is inherently safer than startups because of larger severance packages
Employees should avoid using company tools for any personal productivity work to prevent data collection
In the AI era, the work output and behavioral patterns of knowledge workers are a form of valuable data — and understanding who owns and benefits from that data matters
The best career protection against AI displacement is to specialize in fields with strong unions
knowledgeChecks.explanation: The Meta case highlights that knowledge workers' work patterns — how they debug code, structure emails, reason through problems — have economic value as AI training material. As AI capabilities grow, the implicit data generated by professional work becomes an asset. Understanding what happens to that data, who profits from it, and what rights you retain is becoming a non-trivial part of professional decision-making in the AI era.
